description: Claim-evidence mapping for CRR: Causal Robustness Radii: Certified Perturbation Bounds for Causal Effect Estimates

claims.statement: Pearl (2009): Causal inference requires encoding domain knowledge as a DAG.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: pearl2009 (https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511803161)

claims.statement: Spirtes, Glymour, Scheines (2000): Causation, Prediction, and Search. PC algorithm learns CPDAGs from CI tests.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: spirtes2000 (https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/1754.001.0001)

claims.statement: VanderWeele & Ding (2017): E-value quantifies minimum confounding strength to explain away association.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: vanderweele2017 (https://doi.org/10.7326/M16-2607)

claims.statement: Cinelli & Hazlett (2020): sensemakr extends omitted variable bias with covariate-calibrated sensitivity curves.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: cinelli2020 (https://doi.org/10.1111/rssb.12348)

claims.statement: Maathuis, Kalisch, Bühlmann (2009): IDA computes causal effect bounds within the Markov equivalence class.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: maathuis2009 (https://doi.org/10.1214/09-AOS685)

claims.statement: Peters & Bühlmann (2015): Structural intervention distance (SID) measures interventional disagreement between DAGs.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: peters2015 (https://doi.org/10.1162/NECO_a_00708)

claims.statement: Chickering (2002): GES maximizes a BIC-like score via greedy equivalence search for optimal structure identification.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: chickering2002 (https://www.jmlr.org/papers/v3/chickering02b.html)

claims.statement: Zheng et al. (2018): DAGs with NO TEARS formulates structure learning as continuous optimization.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: zheng2018 (https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2018/hash/e347c51419ffb23ca3fd5050202f9c3d-Abstract.html)

claims.statement: Shah & Peters (2020): No nonparametric conditional independence test has non-trivial power uniformly.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: shah2020 (https://doi.org/10.1214/19-AOS1857)

claims.statement: Liu & Xie (2020): Cauchy combination test with analytic p-value calculation under arbitrary dependency structures.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: liu2020 (https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.2018.1554485)

claims.statement: Drton, Sturmfels, Sullivant (2008): Lectures on Algebraic Statistics — CI testing for DAG model checking.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: drton2008 (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-7643-8905-5)

claims.statement: Masten & Poirier (2020): Breakdown frontiers combine structural and parametric uncertainty.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: masten2020 (https://doi.org/10.3982/QE1288)

claims.statement: Duchi (2021): Distributional robustness provides worst-case guarantees under f-divergence balls.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: duchi2021 (NOT_FOUND — This is a Stanford course reader (Statistics 311/EE 377), not a published paper with a DOI. See https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats311/)
references: duchi2021 (NOT_FOUND — This is a Stanford course reader (Statistics 311/EE 377), not a published paper with a DOI. See https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats311/)

claims.statement: Rosenbaum (2002): Observational Studies, foundational text on sensitivity analysis in observational research.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: rosenbaum2002 (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4757-3692-2)

claims.statement: Lauritzen & Spiegelhalter (1988): Local computations with probabilities on graphical structures. Introduces Asia network.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: lauritzen1988 (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2517-6161.1988.tb01721.x)

claims.statement: Sachs et al. (2005): Causal protein-signaling networks derived from multiparameter single-cell data.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: sachs2005 (https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1105809)

claims.statement: LaLonde (1986): Evaluating econometric evaluations of training programs with experimental data.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: lalonde1986 (https://doi.org/10.2307/1806062)

claims.statement: Hill (2011): Bayesian nonparametric modeling for causal inference. Source of IHDP benchmark.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: hill2011 (https://doi.org/10.1198/jcgs.2010.08162)

claims.statement: Hernán & Robins (2020): Causal Inference: What If. Source of M-bias and backdoor DAG examples.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: hernan2020 (https://www.hsph.harvard.edu/miguel-hernan/causal-inference-book/)

claims.statement: Tennant et al. (2021): Use of DAGs to identify confounders in applied health research.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: tennant2021 (https://doi.org/10.1093/ije/dyaa213)

claims.statement: Robertson & Seymour (1986): Graph minors II — algorithmic aspects of tree-width.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: robertson1986 (https://doi.org/10.1016/0196-6774(86)90023-4)

claims.statement: Bodlaender (1996): Linear-time algorithm for finding tree-decompositions of small treewidth.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: bodlaender1996 (https://doi.org/10.1137/S0097539793251219)

claims.statement: Benjamini & Yekutieli (2001): FDR control under dependency. Used for multiplicity control of CI tests.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: benjamini2001 (https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1013699998)

claims.statement: Zhang et al. (2012): Kernel-based conditional independence test (KCI) and its application in causal discovery.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: zhang2012 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1202.3775)

claims.statement: Robins, Rotnitzky, Zhao (1994): AIPW estimation of regression coefficients when some regressors are not always observed.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: robins1994 (https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1994.10476818)

claims.statement: Chernozhukov et al. (2018): Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: chernozhukov2018 (https://doi.org/10.1111/ectj.12097)

claims.statement: Definition 3.1 (Causal DAG): A causal DAG is G = (V, E) where E is a set of directed edges such that G is acyclic, and P satisfies the Markov property.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Definition 3.2 (Structural Edit): A structural edit is an edge addition (if acyclic), deletion, or reversal (if acyclic). Edit distance is the minimum number of edits to transform one DAG into another.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Definition 3.3 (Conclusion Predicate): A Boolean function Phi(G, P, X, Y) evaluating whether a causal conclusion holds, including identifiability, sign, and significance.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Definition 3.4 (Structural Robustness Radius): rho(G, X, Y, Phi) = min edit distance to a DAG G' where the conclusion Phi fails.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Definition 3.5 (Fragility Score): F(e) in [0,1] is a weighted combination of d-separation impact, identification impact, and estimation impact.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Definition 3.6 (Minimum-Edit DAG Repair): Given DAG G, CI constraints C, and integer k, does there exist a DAG G' with edit distance <= k that is consistent with C?
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 4.1: MEDR is NP-complete, even when restricted to DAGs with maximum in-degree 2.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 4.2: MEDR is fixed-parameter tractable in treewidth w of the moral graph, with running time O(2^{O(w^2)} * |An(X,Y)|^{O(w)}).
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 4.3: The LP relaxation of the ILP formulation for MEDR has integrality gap at most 2w+1, where w is the treewidth of the moral graph.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 4.4 (Locality): The robustness radius depends only on the ancestors of {X,Y}, restricting search from O(p^2) to O(|An(X,Y)|^2) potential edits.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 4.5 (Cauchy Combination Validity): The Cauchy combination of individually valid p-values yields a valid combined p-value under any dependence structure.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 4.6 (Incremental d-Separation): After a single edge edit, all changed CI relations can be identified in O(p * d_max) time.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: CausalCert is implemented as an open-source Python package.
claims.evidence: The implementation/ directory contains a full Python package with pyproject.toml, 15 subpackages (dag, ci_testing, solver, fragility, estimation, data, pipeline, reporting, evaluation, treewidth, benchmarks, simulation, algebra, utils, formats), and 167 Python files totaling ~81,991 lines of code.

claims.statement: CausalCert has [TBD] lines of code across [TBD] subpackages.
claims.evidence: Measured via `find causalcert -name '*.py' | xargs wc -l`: 81,991 total lines across 15 subpackages (dag, ci_testing, solver, fragility, estimation, data, pipeline, reporting, evaluation, treewidth, benchmarks, simulation, algebra, utils, formats) in 167 Python files. The paper uses [TBD] placeholders; the actual values are ~82K LoC and 15 subpackages.

claims.statement: Module 'dag' provides graph operations, d-separation, MEC computation (ALG 1, ALG 2).
claims.evidence: Contains 7,284 LoC across files: graph.py, dsep.py, incremental.py (incremental d-sep for ALG 2), ancestors.py (ALG 1 ancestral set), edit.py, mec.py (Markov equivalence class), moral.py, paths.py, validation.py, causal_order.py, conversions.py, equivalence.py, sparse.py, bitset_dsep.py.

claims.statement: Module 'ci_testing' provides CI tests and multiplicity control (ALG 6).
claims.evidence: Contains 11,154 LoC across files: ensemble.py (Cauchy combination for ALG 6), partial_corr.py, kci.py (kernel CI), rank.py, nonparametric.py, parametric.py, hsic.py, crt.py, multiplicity.py (BY FDR correction), power.py, adaptive.py, base.py, cache.py, classifier.py, diagnostics.py, kernel_ops.py, mutual_info.py, warm_start.py.

claims.statement: Module 'solver' provides ILP, LP, FPT, CDCL solvers (ALG 4, 5, 7, 8).
claims.evidence: Contains 10,517 LoC across files: ilp.py (ALG 4), lp_relaxation.py (ALG 5), fpt.py (ALG 7), cdcl.py (ALG 8), search.py (auto-selection and orchestration), bounds.py, branching.py, constraints.py, cutting_planes.py, heuristics.py, parallel_search.py, phase_saving.py, preprocessing.py, restart_strategy.py, symmetry.py, watched_literals.py, clause_database.py, conflict_analysis.py.

claims.statement: Module 'fragility' provides per-edge scoring and ranking (ALG 3).
claims.evidence: Contains 4,385 LoC across files: scorer.py (main fragility scorer), channels.py (d-sep, identification, estimation channels), ranking.py, aggregation.py, bootstrap.py, bfs.py, theoretical.py.

claims.statement: Module 'estimation' provides AIPW and back-door estimation.
claims.evidence: Contains 9,486 LoC across files: aipw.py, backdoor.py, adjustment.py, crossfit.py, dml.py, effects.py, influence.py, ipw.py, mediation.py, outcome.py, propensity.py, semiparametric.py, sensitivity.py, tmle.py, bounds.py, diagnostics.py, variance.py.

claims.statement: Module 'data' provides CSV/Parquet I/O and synthesis.
claims.evidence: Contains 3,333 LoC for data loading, format conversion, and synthetic data generation.

claims.statement: Module 'pipeline' provides orchestration, CLI, and caching.
claims.evidence: Contains 6,204 LoC across files: orchestrator.py (CausalCertPipeline class), config.py (PipelineRunConfig), cli.py, api.py, cache.py, batch.py, parallel.py, profiler.py, logging_config.py, diagnostics.py.

claims.statement: Module 'reporting' provides JSON/HTML/LaTeX report generation.
claims.evidence: Contains 3,018 LoC across files: json_report.py, html_report.py, latex_report.py, audit.py, interactive.py, narrative.py, visualization.py, plus a templates/ directory.

claims.statement: Module 'evaluation' provides benchmarks, DGPs, and ablation support.
claims.evidence: Contains 6,382 LoC for running benchmarks, evaluating results, and supporting ablation studies.

claims.statement: Module 'treewidth' provides tree decomposition computation.
claims.evidence: Contains 4,393 LoC across files: decomposition.py, bags.py, dp.py (dynamic programming on tree decomposition), elimination.py, nice.py (nice tree decomposition), separator.py, types.py, protocols.py.

claims.statement: Module 'formats' provides DAG format parsers (DOT, GML).
claims.evidence: Contains 4,657 LoC across files: dot_parser.py, gml.py, dagitty_parser.py, tetrad.py, conversion.py, registry.py, validation.py, types.py, protocols.py.

claims.statement: CausalCert provides [TBD] solver backends.
claims.evidence: The SolverStrategy enum defines 5 solver backends: ILP (exact integer linear program), LP_RELAXATION (LP relaxation for fast lower bounds), FPT (fixed-parameter tractable DP on tree decomposition), CDCL (conflict-driven clause-learning search), and AUTO (automatic selection based on graph properties). The paper uses [TBD]; the actual count is 5.

claims.statement: The AUTO solver strategy selects based on graph properties: p<=20 and k_max<=5 → ILP; treewidth w<=8 → FPT; otherwise → LP relaxation + CDCL.
claims.evidence: The search.py file implements the RadiusSolver class with a _select_strategy method that implements the auto-selection logic. For small DAGs (n<=20), FPT is tried first, then ILP. For low treewidth (w<=max_treewidth, default 8), FPT is preferred. Otherwise, LP relaxation provides a lower bound and ILP/CDCL provides upper bounds.

claims.statement: The architecture follows a pipeline design with typed dataclasses at every interface boundary.
claims.evidence: The types.py file (714 LoC) defines typed dataclasses and enums for all interface boundaries: SolverStrategy, CITestMethod, PipelineConfig, and other types. Additional type files exist in subpackages (formats/types.py, treewidth/types.py, simulation/types.py, algebra/types.py). The pipeline orchestrator in pipeline/orchestrator.py uses these types to connect modules.

claims.statement: CausalCert provides a CLI with commands: causalcert audit, causalcert fragility.
claims.evidence: The cli.py file implements CLI commands for CausalCert. The paper shows example CLI usage for 'causalcert audit' (full structural audit with --dag, --data, --treatment, --outcome, --output, --format flags) and 'causalcert fragility' (quick fragility scan with --top-k).

claims.statement: CausalCert provides a Python API via CausalCertPipeline and PipelineRunConfig.
claims.evidence: The quickstart example in the paper imports CausalCertPipeline from causalcert.pipeline.orchestrator and PipelineRunConfig from causalcert.pipeline.config. These classes are implemented in the respective files. The API accepts adjacency matrices, data DataFrames, and configuration objects.

claims.statement: ALG 1: Ancestral Set Computation — standard BFS/DFS from {X,Y} following parent edges, O(|V|+|E|).
claims.evidence: Ancestral set computation is implemented in ancestors.py. Uses BFS/DFS from treatment and outcome nodes following parent edges. Runtime is linear in the size of the graph.

claims.statement: ALG 2: Incremental d-Separation — recompute only affected CI relations after a single edge edit.
claims.evidence: Incremental d-separation is implemented in incremental.py. After a single edge edit, only the CI relations affected by variables on paths through the edited nodes are recomputed, avoiding O(p^2) full recomputation.

claims.statement: ALG 3: Per-Edge Fragility Scorer — computes fragility across d-separation, identification, and estimation channels.
claims.evidence: The main fragility scorer is in scorer.py, with channel implementations in channels.py. For each edge in An(X,Y), it computes: (1) d-sep channel using incremental d-sep (ALG 2), (2) identification channel checking back-door criterion changes, (3) estimation channel measuring ATE change.

claims.statement: ALG 4: ILP Formulation for robustness radius with binary variables, CI-consistency, and acyclicity constraints.
claims.evidence: The ILP solver is implemented in ilp.py. Uses binary variables x_e for each potential edit, topological ordering variables y_ij for acyclicity, CI-consistency constraints, and an edit budget constraint. Solved using Python-MIP with CBC backend.

claims.statement: ALG 5: LP Relaxation — relax binary to continuous for anytime lower bound.
claims.evidence: The LP relaxation solver is in lp_relaxation.py. Relaxes x_e from {0,1} to [0,1]. The fractional optimum provides a lower bound: ceil(z*_LP) <= radius. Integrality gap bounded by O(w) per Theorem 4.3.

claims.statement: ALG 6: Cauchy Combination Ensemble — combines multiple CI tests with complementary power profiles.
claims.evidence: Ensemble CI testing is in ensemble.py. Runs m CI tests (partial correlation for linear, rank-based for monotone, kernel CI for nonlinear), combines p-values via Cauchy combination T_C = sum(w_i * tan(pi(0.5 - p_i))), then applies Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR correction.

claims.statement: ALG 7: FPT-DP on Tree Decomposition — bottom-up DP over tree decomposition of moral graph.
claims.evidence: FPT dynamic programming is in fpt.py. Computes tree decomposition via min-fill heuristic (using treewidth/ module), falls back to LP if width > 8, otherwise enumerates edit configurations per bag and propagates minimum costs bottom-up.

claims.statement: ALG 8: CDCL-Inspired Conflict-Driven Search — maintains partial assignment, propagates forced edits, learns no-good clauses.
claims.evidence: CDCL search is in cdcl.py with supporting files: clause_database.py, conflict_analysis.py, watched_literals.py, phase_saving.py, restart_strategy.py. Maintains partial edit assignments, propagates forced edits from CI constraints, extracts no-good clauses on conflict, and backtracks.

claims.statement: If treewidth > 8, fall back to LP relaxation + heuristic upper bounds.
claims.evidence: The search.py RadiusSolver._select_strategy method implements this logic. For DAGs with moral graph treewidth exceeding the threshold (default 8), the FPT solver is not used; instead LP relaxation provides lower bounds and ILP/CDCL provide upper bounds.

claims.statement: CausalCert is the first tool for systematically stress-testing causal DAGs against structural misspecification.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_first_tool

claims.statement: No existing tool computes the minimum number of structural edits to the DAG that would overturn a specific causal conclusion.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_no_existing_tool_structural_edits

claims.statement: CausalCert is complementary to E-values: structural robustness vs. parametric robustness. An analyst should use both.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_complementary_to_evalue

claims.statement: All experiments use synthetic data from linear-Gaussian SEMs with edge weights in [0.3, 0.9], standard-normal noise, n=2000, alpha=0.05, seed=42.
claims.evidence: [SIMULATED] Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.
claims.audit_note: No benchmark results data file exists; empirical claims cannot be verified

claims.statement: Experiments were run on a single core of a Mac with Apple Silicon.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_apple_silicon

claims.statement: CausalCert relies on the faithfulness assumption for CI testing. Near-faithfulness violations can cause false negatives.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_faithfulness_limitation

claims.statement: The current version supports back-door identification only. Front-door and IV strategies are planned extensions.
claims.evidence: The estimation module contains backdoor.py as the primary identification strategy. While there are files for other estimation approaches (ipw.py, aipw.py, dml.py, tmle.py), the fragility scorer's identification channel checks the back-door criterion specifically. Front-door and IV identification are not implemented in the fragility scoring pipeline.

claims.statement: The ILP solver is practical for p <= [TBD].
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_ilp_scalability_limit

claims.statement: All robustness radii in all experiments are [0,0].
claims.evidence: [SIMULATED] Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.
claims.audit_note: No benchmark results data file exists; empirical claims cannot be verified

claims.statement: CausalCert implements eight numbered algorithms.
claims.evidence: The 8 algorithms are: ALG 1 (Ancestral Set, dag/ancestors.py), ALG 2 (Incremental d-Sep, dag/incremental.py), ALG 3 (Fragility Scorer, fragility/scorer.py), ALG 4 (ILP, solver/ilp.py), ALG 5 (LP Relaxation, solver/lp_relaxation.py), ALG 6 (Cauchy Ensemble, ci_testing/ensemble.py), ALG 7 (FPT-DP, solver/fpt.py), ALG 8 (CDCL, solver/cdcl.py). All have corresponding source files.

claims.statement: FPT-DP falls back to LP relaxation when tree decomposition width > 8.
claims.evidence: The _select_strategy method uses a max_treewidth parameter (default in comments suggests 5-8 range). The ALG 7 description in the paper states width > 8 triggers fallback. The solver/search.py implements this gating logic.

claims.statement: Apply Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR correction across all tested CI relations.
claims.evidence: The multiplicity.py file implements FDR correction methods including Benjamini-Yekutieli (BY), which controls FDR under arbitrary dependence. This is applied after the Cauchy combination test across all CI relations.

claims.statement: The ensemble runs partial correlation (linear), rank-based (monotone), and kernel CI with Nystrom approximation (smooth nonlinear).
claims.evidence: Three CI test families are implemented: partial_corr.py (linear/Gaussian assumptions), rank.py (rank-based, monotone relationships), and kci.py (kernel conditional independence with Nystrom approximation for nonlinear relationships). The ensemble.py orchestrates combining their p-values.

claims.statement: CausalCert's bootstrap-based approach showed moderate performance (-0.260 correlation), with negative correlation suggesting bootstrap stability captures structural stability rather than fragility.
claims.evidence: [SIMULATED] Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.
claims.audit_note: No benchmark results data file exists; empirical claims cannot be verified

claims.statement: IDA searches within the Markov equivalence class; CausalCert searches the k-edit neighborhood which may span multiple MECs.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_ida_searches_within_mec

claims.statement: SID is conclusion-agnostic; CausalCert is conclusion-specific.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_sid_conclusion_agnostic

claims.statement: Standard benchmarks: 0/11 pass. All 11 canonical graphs (chain-5, chain-10, fork, collider, mediator, IV, backdoor, frontdoor, diamond, napkin, bow) produce radius=[0,0], failing to match expected nonzero ranges.
claims.evidence: Benchmark run output: n_benchmarks=11, n_pass=0, all radius lower_bound=0, upper_bound=0.

version_history.2.0: Converted to structured claim-evidence format with typed claims, evidence chains, and verification status

pearl2009.claim: Pearl (2009): Causal inference requires encoding domain knowledge as a DAG.

spirtes2000.claim: Spirtes, Glymour, Scheines (2000): Causation, Prediction, and Search. PC algorithm learns CPDAGs from CI tests.

vanderweele2017.claim: VanderWeele & Ding (2017): E-value quantifies minimum confounding strength to explain away association.

cinelli2020.claim: Cinelli & Hazlett (2020): sensemakr extends omitted variable bias with covariate-calibrated sensitivity curves.

maathuis2009.claim: Maathuis, Kalisch, Bühlmann (2009): IDA computes causal effect bounds within the Markov equivalence class.

peters2015.claim: Peters & Bühlmann (2015): Structural intervention distance (SID) measures interventional disagreement between DAGs.

chickering2002.claim: Chickering (2002): GES maximizes a BIC-like score via greedy equivalence search for optimal structure identification.

zheng2018.claim: Zheng et al. (2018): DAGs with NO TEARS formulates structure learning as continuous optimization.

shah2020.claim: Shah & Peters (2020): No nonparametric conditional independence test has non-trivial power uniformly.

liu2020.claim: Liu & Xie (2020): Cauchy combination test with analytic p-value calculation under arbitrary dependency structures.

drton2008.claim: Drton, Sturmfels, Sullivant (2008): Lectures on Algebraic Statistics — CI testing for DAG model checking.

masten2020.claim: Masten & Poirier (2020): Breakdown frontiers combine structural and parametric uncertainty.

duchi2021.claim: Duchi (2021): Distributional robustness provides worst-case guarantees under f-divergence balls.
duchi2021.url: NOT_FOUND — This is a Stanford course reader (Statistics 311/EE 377), not a published paper with a DOI. See https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats311/

rosenbaum2002.claim: Rosenbaum (2002): Observational Studies, foundational text on sensitivity analysis in observational research.

lauritzen1988.claim: Lauritzen & Spiegelhalter (1988): Local computations with probabilities on graphical structures. Introduces Asia network.

sachs2005.claim: Sachs et al. (2005): Causal protein-signaling networks derived from multiparameter single-cell data.

lalonde1986.claim: LaLonde (1986): Evaluating econometric evaluations of training programs with experimental data.

hill2011.claim: Hill (2011): Bayesian nonparametric modeling for causal inference. Source of IHDP benchmark.

hernan2020.claim: Hernán & Robins (2020): Causal Inference: What If. Source of M-bias and backdoor DAG examples.

tennant2021.claim: Tennant et al. (2021): Use of DAGs to identify confounders in applied health research.

robertson1986.claim: Robertson & Seymour (1986): Graph minors II — algorithmic aspects of tree-width.

bodlaender1996.claim: Bodlaender (1996): Linear-time algorithm for finding tree-decompositions of small treewidth.

benjamini2001.claim: Benjamini & Yekutieli (2001): FDR control under dependency. Used for multiplicity control of CI tests.

zhang2012.claim: Zhang et al. (2012): Kernel-based conditional independence test (KCI) and its application in causal discovery.

robins1994.claim: Robins, Rotnitzky, Zhao (1994): AIPW estimation of regression coefficients when some regressors are not always observed.

chernozhukov2018.claim: Chernozhukov et al. (2018): Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters.
